evidence_quotes.quote: the servers were switched off and the video game was therefore no longer playable.
Manufacturers should therefore be prohibited from using only the manufacturer’s software and from allowing alternative ones.
It should therefore be possible to use purchased digital products without the manufacturer/seller’s dependencies.

paragraphs.text: Special attention should be paid here to 3 additional points, which give rise to massive restrictions as users. 1. Unfortunately, when you buy a digital product, you depend on the manufacturer/seller to continue to make the product available. Should he no longer do so for any reason, access to the purchased product is lost without any other means. In addition, additional measures are often taken to ensure that, as buyers, one cannot gain control over the purchased digital product, such as DRM (Digital Rights Management), unnecessary dependencies on the servers of the manufacturer/seller or specific copying protection software. In other words, one is caught in a dependent position without being able to change this. This is the case, for example, for films bought in digitally, music, video games or software. In particular, there are cases where sellers have removed purchased digital films from the libraries from users or, in the case of video games, where the servers were switched off and the video game was therefore no longer playable. It should therefore be possible to use purchased digital products without the manufacturer/seller’s dependencies. 2. Hardware often requires software from the manufacturer for this functionality, even though there would be no need for software or simpler alternative software, such as other software, open standards or the manual operation of the hardware. However, hardware manufacturers often block access to alternative software or use their own proprietary protocols, which means that the manufacturer’s software must be used. This creates a dependency on the manufacturer’s software without alternative alternatives. If the manufacturer no longer offers the software (my point 1) by removing functions through updates or requiring additional numbers to be used, there is no choice but to consider the manufacturer’s intention. In the worst case scenario, this would create a new e-Waste, as the user of this hardware can only use or wants to use it. Manufacturers should therefore be prohibited from using only the manufacturer’s software and from allowing alternative ones. In addition, with the software, this is the case for network and, in particular, for smart home devices with a manufacturer-specific app. For all purely network devices, there should be a simple possibility to access and use the device directly via the IPs procured in the network via a browser meeting, as only a simple web server is running on most of these devices only technically, but obliges the manufacturers of these devices to use the app. 3. Unfortunately, in the case of purchased software or software which is necessary for hardware (my point 2), it is also often the case that it is provided only on a specific operating system or that it is prohibited from operating on another operating system. Therefore, if one wants to use this software, and thus hardware, often, it is forced to use a specific operating system. This is often the case for graphics applications, video games or hardware software. Manufacturers should therefore be able to make software available for the common operating systems and should not be able to prohibit their operation on other operating systems, so that they are free to choose their operating system without any restrictions.
